prompt: Which work in the corpus identifies each of the five Tathāgatas with a specific state of the practitioner's breath — naming the breath in the right nostril as Amitābha, the breath in the left as Amoghasiddhi, the breath exiting both at once as Ratnasambhava, the breath not exiting from either side as Akṣobhya, and the culmination of the breath as Vairocana — and calls these five 'the fivefold culmination of stainless purity' or 'the Five Sacred Tastes,' locating their abodes at the tip of the nose, the forehead, and the crown of the head? Quote the five-line breath-to-Tathāgata mapping verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The pranayama-based assignment of the five Tathāgatas to right-breath / left-breath / both-breaths / no-breath / breath-culmination, capped with the label 'Five Sacred Tastes' and the three nasal-cranial abodes, is unique to this Old Javanese Mahāyāna manual; the other Old Javanese Buddhist works (Kuñjarakarṇa, Kalpabuddha, San Hyaṅ Mahājñāna, San Hyaṅ Tattvajñāna) and the Saiva texts (Vrhaspatitattva, Gaṇapatitattva) do not contain this specific breath-cycle catalogue of the Pañcatathāgata.

prompt: Which work in the corpus teaches a yogic ritual catalogue in which the breath in the right nostril becomes a triangular fire-maṇḍala, blazing red, with a trident at its center (a means for all actions); or, alternatively, a square Mahendra-maṇḍala blazing gold-yellow with an open five-pronged vajra at its center (for increase of life and wealth); while the breath in the left becomes a formless wind-maṇḍala, black, green, or yellow, blazing at both ends, with a banner-symbol and a hook and ring in the left hand for attraction, immobilization, and expulsion; or, alternatively, a circular white water-maṇḍala (bāruṇamaṇḍala) with a pure-crystal subtle maṇḍala at its center for pacification (śāntika)? Quote the descriptions of the fire-maṇḍala and the water-maṇḍala verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: This precise pairing of nostril (right vs left) with element-maṇḍala (fire / Mahendra / wind / water), each with a specific shape, color, central symbol (trident, five-pronged vajra, banner+hook+ring, crystal), and specific siddhi outcome (sarvakarma, life-and-wealth increase, ākarṣaṇa-sthāmbana-uccāraṇa, śāntika), is unique to this Old Javanese Mahāyāna manual; no other Old Javanese Buddhist or Saiva work in the corpus, and none of the Sanskrit smṛtis or non-Indic works, contains this specific breath-element-maṇḍala-siddhi correspondence.

prompt: Which work in the corpus, in defining the supreme grade of giving (above ordinary giving and above the giving of one's wife and child), tells of the Mahāsattva who gave his flesh and blood as a gift to demons, tigers, and garuḍas; gave his eyes as a gift to an old, blind brahmin who came to test his heroic generosity; and gave his heart as a gift to a suffering, sorrowful creature — illustrating a verse that says one who has given one's own flesh and blood and conquered the lord of demons, the heart, and the body, and through the power of giving experiences no pain, is what the sages proclaim 'mahātidāna'? Quote the verse and the three specific recipients (demons-tigers-garuḍas / old blind brahmin / suffering creature) verbatim and identify the work and chapter.
rationale: The threefold dāna hierarchy (dāna / atidāna / mahātidāna) culminating in the Mahāsattva's flesh-to-demons-tigers-garuḍas, eyes-to-blind-brahmin-as-test, and heart-to-suffering-creature triad, illustrating the specific śloka about conquering 'the lord of demons, the heart, the body' through giving, is uniquely framed in this Mahāyāna pāramitā exposition; the other Old Javanese Buddhist narratives (Kuñjarakarṇa, Kalpabuddha) tell different jātaka-style episodes, and no Sanskrit smṛti, Old English religious poem, or European treatise in the corpus catalogues the perfection of giving with this specific verse-and-three-recipient structure.
